coredata.dc:title: Synergy of mesoporous SnO2 and RbF modification for high-efficiency and stable perovskite solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Science Press and Dalian Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by ELSEVIER B.V. and Science Press.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Qian
Peng, Changtao
Du, Lin
Hou, Tian
Yu, Wenjing
Chen, Dong
Shu, Hui
Huang, Dejun
Zhou, Xiangqing
Zhang, Jinyang
Zhang, Wenfeng
Li, Haijin
Xie, Jiale
Huang, Yuelong
coredata.dc:description: Electron transport layer (ETL) is very critical to the performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs), and optimization work on ETL has received extensive attentions especially on tin oxide (SnO2) since it is an excellent ETL material widely applied in high-efficiency PSCs. Thereinto, introducing mesoporous structure and surface modification are two important approaches which are commonly applied. Herein, based on the previous work in low-temperature fabrication process of mesoporous SnO2 (m-SnO2), we introduced a modification process with rubidium fluoride (RbF) to the m-SnO2 ETL, and successfully achieved a synergy of the m-SnO2 and RbF modification: not only the shortcoming of the m-SnO2 in interfacial traps was overcome, but also the carrier collection efficiency was further improved. The PSCs based on the m-SnO2 ETL with RbF modification demonstrated outstanding performances: a champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 22.72% and a stability performance of maintaining 90% of the initial PCE after 300 h of MPP tracking were obtained without surface passivation of perovskite film. Hence, utilizing the abovementioned synergy is a cost-effective and feasible strategy for fabricating high-efficiency and stable PSCs since the fabrication process of the m-SnO2 ETL is a kind of low temperature process and RbF is cheap.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tin oxide
Mesoporous structure
Rubidium fluoride
Perovskite solar cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was mainly supported by the operational funds of Institute of Photovoltaic, Southwest Petroleum University and Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2018JY0015). We thank H.M. Li from Southwest Petroleum University for helping with the EQE measurements, and thank B. Cai from Sichuan Research Center of New Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics for helping with the step profiler measurements.

This work was mainly supported by the operational funds of Institute of Photovoltaic, Southwest Petroleum University and Sichuan Science and Technology Program (2018JY0015). We thank H.M. Li from Southwest Petroleum University for helping with the EQE measurements, and thank B. Cai from Sichuan Research Center of New Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics for helping with the step profiler measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM images of SnO2 films with different structures and modification processes. (a) planar SnO2 film (p-SnO2); (b) RbF doped planar SnO2 film (p-SnO2-RbF); (c) mesoporous SnO2 film (m-SnO2); (d) mesoporous SnO2 film with surface RbF modification (m-SnO2/RbF).
Schematical device structures of the PSCs based on different ETL substrates. (a) p-SnO2; (b) p-SnO2-RbF; (c) m-SnO2; (d) m-SnO2/RbF.
(a) XPS spectra and (b) XPS of Sn 3d core-level spectra for different ETLs.
Photovoltaic parameters of the PSCs based on different ETLs extracted from the J-V tests: (a) V OC; (b) J SC; (c) FF, (d) PCE.
(a) Typical J-V curves and (b)–(d) the J-V curves of forward scan and reverse scan of different ETL-based PSCs from − 0.2 to 1.2 V.

(a) The EQEs and corresponding integrated current densities of PSCs based on different ETLs; (b) the UV absorption spectra of the perovskite films based on different ETLs; (c) I-V characteristic curves for the different ETLs with an ITO/ETL/Ag structure; (d) the Nyquist plots of the PSCs based on different ETLs.
(a) The dark I-V curves from the single-carrier limit current (SCLC) tests for the devices based on (a) the p-SnO2 ETL and p-SnO2-RbF ETL and (b) the m-SnO2 ETL and m-SnO2/RbF ETL.
(a) The steady state PL spectra and (b) the time-resolved PL decay spectra for the perovskite films based on different ETLs.
The MPP tracking for 300 h on the PSCs based on p-SnO2 ETL and m-SnO2/RbF ETL under continuous AM 1.5 G illumination (simulated by LED light).
The maximum and average values of photovoltaic parameters of perovskite solar cells of different ETLs.

head.ce:title: Synergy of mesoporous SnO2 and RbF modification for high-efficiency and stable perovskite solar cells

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Photovoltaic, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, Sichuan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Photovoltaic, Southwest Petroleum University, Chengdu 610500, Sichuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This graph demonstrates the photovoltaic performance comparison between the PSCs based on the p-SnO2 ETL and m-SnO2/RbF ETL, and schematically shows how RbF modification affects the interface of m-SnO2/perovskite film.

Electron transport layer (ETL) is very critical to the performance of perovskite solar cells (PSCs), and optimization work on ETL has received extensive attentions especially on tin oxide (SnO2) since it is an excellent ETL material widely applied in high-efficiency PSCs. Thereinto, introducing mesoporous structure and surface modification are two important approaches which are commonly applied. Herein, based on the previous work in low-temperature fabrication process of mesoporous SnO2 (m-SnO2), we introduced a modification process with rubidium fluoride (RbF) to the m-SnO2 ETL, and successfully achieved a synergy of the m-SnO2 and RbF modification: not only the shortcoming of the m-SnO2 in interfacial traps was overcome, but also the carrier collection efficiency was further improved. The PSCs based on the m-SnO2 ETL with RbF modification demonstrated outstanding performances: a champion power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 22.72% and a stability performance of maintaining 90% of the initial PCE after 300 h of MPP tracking were obtained without surface passivation of perovskite film. Hence, utilizing the abovementioned synergy is a cost-effective and feasible strategy for fabricating high-efficiency and stable PSCs since the fabrication process of the m-SnO2 ETL is a kind of low temperature process and RbF is cheap.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past few years, the highest power conversion efficiency (PCE) of perovskite solar cells (PSCs) has grown rapidly from 3.8% to 25.5% [1–4], which has attracted wide attention from researchers. The electron transport layer (ETL) plays a role in extracting electrons and blocking holes in the PSCs, which is very critical for the performance of the PSCs. Mesoporous structure is commonly applied in the ETL of PSCs to enhance carrier collection and light absorption due to its high specific surface area. For the most popular ETL materials in high-efficiency PSCs, namely TiO2 [5] and SnO2 [6], the preparation process of mesoporous TiO2 is relatively mature, but requires a very high processing temperature (>450 °C), and mesoporous TiO2 has a poor UV stability [7,8], in contrast, SnO2 has advantages in UV stability, electron mobility and energy level alignment with common perovskite materials. Gräetzel and Hagfeldt group [9] firstly achieved high-efficiency (18.4%) PSCs based on planar SnO2 (p-SnO2) ETL in 2015, and then Roose et al. [10] from the same research group developed mesoporous SnO2 (m-SnO2) ETL based PSCs in 2016 which showed better UV stability over m-TiO2 and even p-SnO2 ETL but lower PCE. Afterwards, the application of the planar SnO2 ETL developed rapidly and widely applied in high-efficiency PSCs [11], and record PCEs of PSCs were achieved continually like You group [12] in 2019 and Yoo et al. [13] in 2021, but unfortunately up to date the progress in application of mesoporous SnO2 is lagging behind due to the difficulties in its preparation process and interface passivation.

In order to exploit the advantages of mesoporous structure ETL in light scattering and carrier collection, intensive efforts have been made and many valuable progresses have been achieved in recent years. Firstly, from the prospective of morphology engineering, many microstructures with high specific surface area like microspheres [14], nanotube arrays [15] and nanorod arrays [16] were developed, and recently Lv et al. [17] reported high-efficiency (22.5%) PSCs based on anatase TiO2 nanopyramid arrays ETLs. From the results reported in the literature, it can be concluded that mesoporous structure ETLs can improve the electron collection efficiency as well as light utilization for high-efficiency PSCs. However, there are still many challenges in preparation method to generate high specific surface area and meanwhile suppress its negative impacts like high trap density at the ETL/perovskite interface [16]. Currently most of the preparation processes for m-SnO2 require high temperature annealing, for example, 500 °C annealing in sol-gel method [18], 450 °C annealing in soft template method [10], and 600 °C annealing in hydrothermal method [19], that is harmful to the properties of the SnO2 film. Even though some low temperature processes have been developed for m-SnO2, film morphology [20] and interfacial deep defects [21] are still the critical issues which limited the performance of the m-SnO2 ETL based PSCs. Last year, our group introduced polyethylene glycol (PEG) into the SnO2 precursor solution and successfully obtained a m-SnO2 ETL through a low-temperature process [22], and in that work it can be clearly seen that the carrier collection efficiency was obviously improved by the application of the m-SnO2 ETL (the short-circuit current (J SC) and fill factor (FF) of the corresponding PSCs were significantly improved), however, the trap density at the ETL/perovskite interface was also increased which caused considerable loss in open circuit voltage (V OC) of the corresponding PSCs. M-SnO2 ETL can introduce high carrier collection efficiency due to its large specific surface area which also brings high interfacial trap density. Therefore, adopting a simple and effective interface passivation strategy to compensate for this shortcoming is crucial for releasing the potential of m-SnO2 ETL.

Generally, modification to SnO2 ETL aiming to enhance electron extraction and reduce trap density at the ETL/perovskite interface is an effective way to improve the performance of PSCs which is commonly applied in the community. For example, specific metal ion (Li+, Nb5+, Eu2+, etc.) doping to SnO2 ETL is beneficial to optimize the energy level of ETL and enhance the collection ability of electrons [23–25], and Sun et al. [26] reported > 20% PCE of PSCs based on antimony-doped SnO2 nanorod arrays. Moreover, some organic molecules (such as EDTA [27], PEG [28], GYD [29]) can form hybrid organic–inorganic composites with SnO2 which help to reduce defect density at the interface and improve the quality of perovskite films, and quantum dots of carbon derivatives (such as GQDs [30] and RCQs [31]) can significantly improve electron mobility of SnO2 ETL and reduce interface charge recombination because quantum dots can generate carriers which transfer to the conduction band of SnO2 and then effectively fill electron traps as well as improve the conductivity of SnO2. In addition, Sargent group [32] used ammonium fluoride (NH4F) to modify the surface of SnO2 ETL aiming to reduce defect density since ammonium ions can eliminate the hydroxyl groups on the surface of SnO2 and F− ion may have interaction with Sn4+ ion [33,34]. Considering that the key issue for the m-SnO2 ETL is the large specific surface area which brings lots of dangling bonds, F− ion could be a good passivation agent for those dangling bonds, and since Rb+ ion is beneficial to the performance of PSCs [35,36], we believe that rubidium fluoride (RbF) is a good candidate of interface passivation agent for the m-SnO2 ETL. Coincidentally, when we were preparing this paper, Mao & Wang's group [37] reported that RbF can modify planar SnO2 ETL to get an excellent interfacial passivation effect.

In this work, we found that RbF could act as an effective modification agent to SnO2 ETL which improves the conductivity of the SnO2 film and passivates the trap defects near the SnO2/perovskite interface because there is a strong interaction between F and Sn atoms, and meanwhile Rb+ ions can diffuse into perovskite film acting as a kind of passivation agent and suppressing ion migration. Since RbF can work well in trap passivation for the SnO2/perovskite interface, we applied RbF to modify the m-SnO2 film intending to compensate for its disadvantages of high interfacial trap density which caused obvious V OC drop. Surprisingly, a synergy of the m-SnO2 and RbF modification was successfully achieved: not only the shortcoming of the m-SnO2 ETL in interfacial traps was overcome by RbF modification, but also the carrier collection efficiency was further improved. Eventually, a champion PCE of 22.72% along with a V OC of 1.139 V, J SC of 24.48 mA cm−2, and FF of 81.5% and a stability performance of maintaining 90% of the initial PCE after 300 h of MPP tracking were obtained, that are outstanding performance for the PSCs without surface passivation of perovskite film. Therefore, an effective strategy through utilizing the abovementioned synergy could be proposed for fabricating high-efficiency and stable PSCs.

The solvent of anhydrous dimethyl formamide (DMF, 99.9%), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), chlorobenzene (CB 99.9%), and isopropanol (IPA 99.9%) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The chemical of lead iodide (PbI2), lead chloride (PbCl2), cesium Iodide (CsI), formamidinium iodide (FAI), methanaminium chlorine (MACl), methanaminium bromide (MABr) and Li-bis (trifluoromethanesulfonyl) imide (Li-TFSI), 4-tert-butylpyridine (tBP) was purchased from Xi'an Polymer Light Technology Corp. RbF was purchased from Aladdin. The 2,2′,7,7′-tetrakis(N,N-dip-methoxyphenylamine)-9,9′-spirobifluorene (Spiro-OMeTAD 99.8%) was purchased from Lumtec. The nanoparticle SnO2 colloid precursor and PEG were purchased from Alfa Aesar. All chemicals including solvents and reagents were used as purchased without further purification.

The prepatterned ITO/glass (1.6 cm × 2 cm) substrates were ultrasonic cleaned by ethanol, isopropanol and ethanol sequentially for 15 min in turn. The ITO substrates were treated by UV-ozone for 15 min before spin coating. For the planar SnO2 layer, the SnO2 precursor solution (1:3 volume ratio of the raw SnO2 colloid solution and deionized water with different concentrations of RbF) was deposited on the cleaned ITO substrates by spin-coating at 4000 r min−1 for 20 s and annealed in ambient air at 180 °C for 20 min. For the mesoporous SnO2 layer, the SnO2 precursor solution (1:3 volume ratio of the raw SnO2 colloid solution and deionized water with 6% volume ratio of PEG) was deposited on the planar SnO2 layer by spin-coating at 5000 r min−1 for 30 s and annealed in ambient air at 180 °C for 20 min, and then treated by UV-ozone for 30 min. For the RbF treatment of the mesoporous SnO2 layer, the RbF solution (10 mg mL−1 in deionized water) was deposited on the mesoporous SnO2 layer by spin-coating at 5000 r min−1 for 30 s and annealed in ambient air at 120 °C for 10 min.

The perovskite film is prepared by a two-step spin coating method: Firstly, the PbI2 precursor mixture solution (PbI2:PbCl2:CsI = 600 mg:9 mg:16.9 mg dissolved in a mixed solvent of DMF (900 μL) and DMSO (100 μL), and then stirred at 70 °C for 12 h) was spin coated on the SnO2/ITO substrate at 1500 r min−1 for 30 s, then annealed at 70 °C for 1 min. Secondly, the FAI precursor mixture solution (FAI:MABr:MACl = 60 mg:6 mg:6 mg dissolved in 1 mL IPA) was spin coated on the PbI2 layer at 1700 r min−1 for 30 s, and then the precursor film was taken out from the glove box immediately and annealed at 150 °C for 15 min in ambient air (30%–40% RH). For the Spiro-OMeTAD HTL layer, the Spiro-OMeTAD mixture solution was spin coated on the perovskite film at 3000 r min−1 for 30 s, where 1 mL Spiro-OMeTAD/chlorobenzene solution contained 72.3 mg Spiro-OMeTAD, 35 μL Li-TFSI solution (520 mg Li-TFSI in 1 mL acetonitrile), 28.8 μL tBP. Finally, the 100 nm silver electrode is obtained by thermal evaporation at a pressure of 2 × 10−4 Pa.

The top-view SEM images were attained by a high-resolution field emission SEM (FEI Quanta 650 FEG, USA). The AFM images were attained by Kelvin probe force microscope (KPFM, KEYSIGHT Technologies 7500, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were gained from the instrument (ESCALAB 250, Thermo, with a monochromatized Al K α X-ray source in vacuum). The J-V characteristics of the photovoltaic cells were obtained using a Keithley B2901A source meter under simulated one-sun AM 1.5 G illumination (100 mW cm−2) with a solar simulator (Enli Tech, SS-F5-3A, Taiwan). The active device area was 0.105 cm2. A 468 nm light and a 406 nm pulsed laser were used as the excitation sources for the steady-state photoluminescence (PL) and time-resolved PL (TRPL) measurement (Edinburgh FLS 980, USK), respectively. The UV–vis spectra were characterized using a UV-Vis-NIR spectrometer (Shimadzu, UV-3600 Plus, Japan). The external quantum efficiency (EQE) spectrum and integral current density were measured by EQE system (BENTHAM TMC300, USK) in the range of the 300–900 nm wavelength at ambient temperature. The EIS was measured by an electrochemical station. The thickness of ETL films with different structures is measured by a step profiler (DektakXT, Germany).

The precursor solution used for the deposition of SnO2 film is a kind of nanoparticle SnO2 colloid dispersion solution (commercial product from Alfa Aesar) which contains lots of SnO2 nanoparticles with size smaller than 5 nm. In order to enhance the modification effect, we added a trace mount of RbF powder into the SnO2 precursor solution, and since RbF can be easily dissolved in water, Rb+ and F− ions can surround the SnO2 nano-grains thoroughly and contact with their surface efficiently (this type of ETL is denoted as SnO2-RbF). It was surprisingly found from experimental results that RbF addictive to SnO2 precursor can not only modify the physical properties of the deposited SnO2 film, but also impact on the surface morphology of the film, that is, the film formation process. For the p-SnO2 film, RbF addictive made the film rougher (it can be seen from the SEM images in Fig. 1 , and AFM characterization also showed higher roughness in Fig. S1); for the m-SnO2 film, the impact of RbF addictive on the film formation process was much more severe: a trace of RbF addiction changed the continuous m-SnO2 film into isolated islands as shown in Fig. S3. So, for the modification of the m-SnO2 film, an ultrathin wet RbF film was spin coated on the pre-deposited m-SnO2 film and then annealed at 120 °C for 10 min (this type of ETL is denoted as m-SnO2/RbF). And this modification method has little impact on the surface morphology of the m-SnO2 film as shown in Fig. 1. In this work, we emphasized on the comparison between the p-SnO2, p-SnO2-RbF, m-SnO2 and m-SnO2/RbF ETLs and their corresponding PSCs which device structures were schematically shown in Fig. 2 .

In order to investigate the interatomic interaction between RbF and SnO2 in the above four type of ETLs, XPS tests were performed and the results are shown in Fig. 3 (a). Compared with the unmodified SnO2 film, the RbF-modified films have new peaks at the binding energy of 684.5 and 110.3 eV, respectively for the F 1 s and Rb 3d peaks (Fig. S4a and b) for both planar and mesoporous structures, indicating that Rb+ and F− ions were successfully bonded onto the SnO2 films. What’s more important, Fig. 3(b) shows that the Sn 3d peak of the RbF-modified SnO2 films has shifted about 0.5 eV to the low binding energy direction, which indicates a strong interaction between F and Sn atoms [38,39]. It is worth noting that the shift of the Sn 3d peak was obviously observed for both the p-SnO2-RbF film and the m-SnO2/RbF film in this work, however, the results reported by Mao & Wang's group [37] shows that this kind of shift was only observed for the SnO2-RbF type of modification, but not for the SnO2/RbF type of modification (there is no annealing in their modification process). We believe that the high surface energy for the m-SnO2 film and annealing in our modification process can promote the interaction between RbF and SnO2. Hence, the modification effect of the m-SnO2/RbF film is also strong as the p-SnO2-RbF film which is confirmed by the following characterization and device tests results.

PSCs based on the four types of ETLs were fabricated to verify the effect of RbF modification to the SnO2 ETLs, and the extracted photovoltaic parameters and typical J-V curves from the J-V tests are shown in Fig. 4 (the corresponding statistical data of maximum and average values for each group are shown in Table 1 ) and Fig. 5 (a), respectively. Just from the comparison between the results for the p-SnO2 ETL and p-SnO2-RbF ETL, the effect of RbF modification is very pronounced: all the parameters (V OC, J SC and FF) were improved obviously, which implies the improvement of the conductivity of the SnO2 film and the carrier collection efficiency, and the passivation of the trap defects near the SnO2/perovskite interface. For the m-SnO2 ETL based PSCs, we got similar results with our previous work [22]: obvious improvements of J SC and FF due to higher electron extraction capability and carrier collection efficiency, but a slight drop of V OC due to higher interfacial trap density. After the RbF modification to the m-SnO2 ETL, V OC and FF were significantly improved as expected, and J SC also got a slight increase. This may mainly be ascribed to the effect of RbF modification in the improvement of the conductivity and the interfacial passivation. It is interesting to compare the results of device performance from p-SnO2-RbF ETL and m-SnO2/RbF ETL, both with RbF modification but different structure of SnO2, that is another focus of this work. The variation of device performance from p-SnO2-RbF ETL to m-SnO2/RbF ETL could be explained as following: (1) a slight drop of V OC and a slight increase of J SC, there is obvious difference in specific surface area and interfacial trap density between the p-SnO2 ETL and m-SnO2 ETL which caused relatively large drop of V OC and increase of J SC, while RbF modification could passivate interfacial traps and increase the conductivity and roughness of the p-SnO2 film so that the difference mentioned above could be nearly counteracted; (2) a remarkable increase of FF which resulted in the considerable improvement of PCE (the champion cell: a PCE of 22.72% along with a V OC of 1.139 V, J SC of 24.48 mA cm−2, and FF of 81.5%), from our previous work and above analysis we know both mesoporous structure and RbF modification could improve FF of the corresponding devices, and here we achieved a valuable progress that is the synergy between mesoporous structure and RbF modification for the SnO2 ETL has been realized: the shortcoming of the mesoporous structure in interfacial traps can be compensated by RbF modification, and the mesoporous structure can further improve carrier collection efficiency even for the SnO2 ETL with RbF modification. Since RbF is very cheap and the modification process is facile, this synergy can help us fully release the potential of the m-SnO2 ETL indicated in our previous work.

In order to verify the J SC values of the PSCs based on the four kinds of ETLs, EQE spectra were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 6 (a). The integrated current densities of PSCs based on p-SnO2, p-SnO2-RbF, m-SnO2, m-SnO2/RbF are 22.36, 23.45, 22.91, and 23.72 mA cm−2, respectively (Table S1), which are consistent with the J SC values from the J-V tests within 5% of the error range. From the EQE spectra the increments of J SC are mainly from the wavelength range of from 300 to 600 nm. Fig. 6(b) shows the UV-visible absorptance spectra for the perovskite films deposited on those four types of ETLs which also demonstrated the increment of light absorption in the wavelength range of from 300 to 600 nm coinciding with the EQE results very well. It illustrates that the increase of surface roughness for the ETLs can contribute to the increments of J SC due to the enhancement of light scattering. Both RbF modification and mesoporous structure can enhance light absorption through increasing surface roughness for the SnO2 ETL.

From the above results the common contributions of RbF modification and mesoporous structure to the performance of PSCs are mainly from the improvement of carrier collection efficiency, so many characterizations were carried out to get a deeper understanding on this point. Firstly, the I-V tests on the devices with a structure of ITO/ETL/Ag were done to evaluate the conductivity of the four types of ETLs, and the results are shown in Fig. 6(c). The following formula (1) was used to calculate their conductivity (σ) [40,41], and the calculated results are shown in Table S2.
(1)
σ =
IL
VA
,

where I/V is the slope of the I-V curve, L is the thickness of the ETL film, and A is the effective area. The results clearly shows that both RbF modification and mesoporous structure can significantly improve the conductivity of the SnO2 ETLs, and they can work together as a synergic effect to get a further improvement of the conductivity: the conductivity of the m-SnO2/RbF film (8.74 × 10−3 mS cm−1), the highest value in the four types of ETLs, is nearly triple more than that of the p-SnO2 film (2.95 × 10−3 mS cm−1).

Besides, the space charge limited current (SCLC) characterization with a single-carrier device structure of ITO/ETL/Perovskite/PCBM/Ag based on the four types of ETLs was used to examine the electron mobility and interfacial trap density of the films. The log(I)–log(V) plots of the pure electron-carrier devices measured under dark conditions are shown in Fig. 7 , which is divided into three areas including ohmic region, trap filling limited (TFL) region and Child’s region [42,43]. The trap density (N trap) and electron mobility (µ) are calculated by the following formula (2) and (3) respectively [42]:
(2)
N
t r a p
=
2
ε 0
ε r
V
T F L
e
L
2
,
(3)
μ =
8
I μ
L
3
9 A
ε 0
ε r
V
μ
2
,
where V TFL can be obtained from the focal point of the linear fitting of the ohmic area and the TFL area, Vµ and Iµ can be obtained from the focal point of the linear fitting of the TFL area and the child area [44], e is the element charge, L is the thickness of the perovskite layer, ε 0 is the vacuum dielectric constant, εr is the relative dielectric constant of the perovskite layer, and A is the effective area of the device. The calculated values of N trap and µ for the devices based on different ETLs are shown in Table S3. As we known, the calculated N trap and µ values actually reflect combination properties of all the films and corresponding interfaces in the SnO2/perovskite/PCBM film stack, and the perovskite layer and corresponding interfaces take the dominant influence. In this work, however, the difference of the samples for the SCLC characterization is only from SnO2 layer and SnO2/perovskite interface, so it is reasonable to acrible the variation of the calculated N trap and µ values to the variation of the properties of SnO2 layer and SnO2/perovskite interface. Surprisingly the variation trend of µ values agrees very well with that of conductivity for the ETLs, even the variation extent is similar: the µ value of the m-SnO2/RbF-based device (1.272 × 10−1 cm2 V−1 s−1) is also about twice of that of the p-SnO2-based device (6.663 × 10−2 cm2 V−1 s−1). This may suggest that the improvement of the conductivity is mainly due to the improvement of the electron mobility. The data of N trap demonstrates that the mesoporous structure introduced higher interfacial trap density due to its larger specific surface area, while the RbF modification could significantly passivate the interfacial traps no matter what modification method was used. The results of the SCLC and conductivity tests are in good consistence with those from J-V tests. It should be pointed out that in this work the electrical properties of the m-SnO2 film were also improved by the type of SnO2/RbF modification while Mao & Wang's group [37] reported almost unchanged electrical properties for the SnO2/RbF ETL compared with the SnO2 ETL. This is consistent with the results of XPS characterization that the strong interaction between F and Sn atoms was found in this work but not in that reported by Mao & Wang's group for the type of SnO2/RbF modification. This may imply that the improvement of the electrical properties for RbF modification to SnO2 film may be mainly ascribed to the formation of Sn–F bonds reducing the density of dangling bonds at the interface which are the main source of the interfacial traps. The density functional theory (DFT) calculations in the previous work reported by Mao & Wang's group [37] demonstrated that there is strong interaction between F and Sn atoms and stronger bonding between the SnO2 ETL and perovskite film after the RbF modification which may also contribute to the improvement of the conductivity. Those DFT calculation results are consistent with the above characterization results and we believe the two effects abovementioned are the main underlying mechanisms for RbF modification to SnO2 ETL to improve the performance of the corresponding PSCs.

In order to further verify the effects of RbF modification to the p-SnO2 and m-SnO2 ETLs, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the steady-state PL spectra and the TRPL decay spectra were measured for the PSCs based on the four types of ETLs. The Nyquist plots and corresponding equivalent circuit diagram for the PSCs are shown in Fig. 6(d). The semicircle in the high frequency region reflects the charge transfer resistance (R ct) which is an indicator of carrier extraction capability, while the semicircle in the low frequency region reflects the recombination resistance (R rec) which is an indicator of recombination speed [38,45]. The fitting results for the Nyquist plots are shown in Table S4. Compared with the p-SnO2 ETL based device, the values of R ct for the other devices are all smaller among which the value of R ct for the m-SnO2/RbF ETL based device is the smallest illustrating that both RbF modification and mesoporous structure can improve carrier extraction capability and they can work together to achieve a synergic effect; As for R rec, the values for the devices based on the SnO2 ETLs with RbF modification are higher than those without RbF modification, and the values for the devices based on the p-SnO2 ETL are higher than that on the m-SnO2 ETL illustrating that mesoporous structure may introduce interfacial traps and RbF modification can passivate the interfacial traps.

Fig. 8 shows (a) the steady-state PL and (b) the TRPL decay spectra of the perovskite films deposited on the four types of ETLs (the excitation light is incident from the glass side). By fitting the TRPL decay spectra which can be fitted well with a biexponential decay function including a fast decay (τ 1) and a slow one (τ 2), the carrier lifetime τ 1 and τ 2 can be calculated and the results are shown in Table S5. Generally speaking, the fast decay τ 1 reflects the recombination process at the ETL/perovskite interface, while the slow decay τ 2 reflects that in the bulk perovskite film [46]. When the sample has double layers of perovskite film with ETL and the excitation light is incident from the glass side, namely the ETL side, the fast decay τ 1 is usually regarded as an indicator of electron extraction speed, and the slow decay τ 2 as an indicator of recombination speed [47]. Looking at the data of τ 1 and τ 2 in this way, we also can draw the similar conclusion as the above characterizations: both RbF modification and mesoporous structure can enhance electron extraction speed and they can work synergistically together (from the variation of τ 1), and mesoporous structure may introduce interfacial traps and RbF modification can passivate the interfacial traps (from the variation of τ 2). The PL spectra in Fig. 8(a) show that mesoporous structure of SnO2 ETL reduced the PL intensity of the perovskite films, but RbF modification enhanced the PL intensity. Since PL intensity reflects a whole image of radiative recombination in perovskite film, as for the mesoporous structure, it enhances electron extraction and introduces interfacial traps, both resulting in PL quenching, so no doubt that it reduced the PL intensity; as for RbF modification, it passivates the interfacial traps which may improve electron extraction by increasing electron mobility but also eliminate nonradiative recombination centers, so the result shows that the latter is dominant which means RbF modification has a strong passivation effect [48]. It is worth noting that after RbF modification to the SnO2 ETLs, the PL peaks of the perovskite films shifted a little bit to the shorter wavelength direction suggesting that Rb+ ions may have diffused into the perovskite film and it can also act a kind of passivation agent to the traps in the perovskite film [37,49].

All the above characterizations and tests on the SnO2 ETLs and corresponding devices show consistent results depicting a clear image about the effects of mesoporous structure and RbF modification, and their synergy on the SnO2-ETL based PSCs: the mesoporous structure of the SnO2-ETL enhances electron extraction and introduces interfacial trap defects due to high specific surface area, and the RbF modification to the SnO2-ETL improves the conductivity of the SnO2-ETL and passivates the interfacial traps at the SnO2/perovskite interface through the formation of Sn-F bonds and the trap defects in the perovskite film by Rb+ ions which get into the perovskite film through diffusion. Since the RbF modification can eliminate most of the interfacial traps introduced by the mesoporous structure and they enhance electron extraction through different ways, they can work together to achieve a synergic effect to further improve the performance of the corresponding PSCs. Hence, the mesoporous SnO2 ETL with RbF modification proposed in this work is an effective strategy to further improve the performance of PSCs.

The important concern about this strategy is its impact on the stability of the PSCs. Firstly, hysteresis factor (HF) for the PSCs based on different ETLs was checked which is calculated by the following formula (4):
(4)
H F =
P C E
r e v e r s e
-
P C E
f o r w a r d
P C E
r e v e r s e
,

where PCEreverse and PCEforward are the PCE from J-V tests of reverse scan and forward scan respectively. Fig. 5(b–d) show the typical J-V curves of reverse and forward scans, and the inset tables show the data of the photovoltaic parameters extracted from the J-V curves and the calculated HFs, illustrating that the RbF modification suppressed the hysteresis in the PSCs. The PSC based on the m-SnO2/RbF ETL has the lowest value of HF, indicating that mesoporous structure and RbF modification for SnO2 ETL help to suppress the hysteresis. It is well-known that reducing charge accumulation at the perovskite/ETL interface by enhancing electron extraction is an effective way to alleviate the hysteresis [50,51], and interstitial Rb+ in the perovskite film can induce steric impediment effect to suppress ion migration [52] which is regarded as one of the main causes for the hysteresis issue. From the above characterizations and analysis, it is easy to understand why mesoporous structure and RbF modification for SnO2 ETL can help to resolve the hysteresis issue.

In addition, to testify the long-term stability of the PSC based on the m-SnO2/RbF ETL, the steady-state output tracking of the maximum power point (MPP) under continuous AM 1.5 G illumination (simulated by LED light) was carried out on a typical sample device together with a reference control sample device based on the p-SnO2 ETL (for both the two samples, Ag electrode was replaced by Au electrode since it is well known that Ag electrode has bad stability). As shown in Fig. 9 , the m-SnO2/RbF ETL based device still maintained 90% of its initial efficiency after tracking MPP for 300 h, in the meantime the efficiency of the p-SnO2 ETL based device degraded to 60% of its initial efficiency. The m-SnO2/RbF ETL based device demonstrated excellent stability and substantially outperformed the p-SnO2 ETL based device. This also may be ascribed to the enhancement of electron extraction which reduces charge accumulation at the interface and Rb+ ion diffusion into the perovskite film to occupy interstitial positions which suppresses ion migration. The results clearly confirmed that the combination of mesoporous structure and RbF modification for SnO2 ETL is beneficial to the stability of PSC devices.

In summary, both mesoporous structure and RbF modification for SnO2 ETL are effective methods to improve the performance of the PSCs. Compared with normal planar structure, mesoporous structure possesses larger specific surface area which can enhance electron collection efficiency, however it also introduces higher interfacial trap density. RbF modification can increase the conductivity of SnO2 ETL and passivate interfacial traps through forming F–Sn bonds, and meanwhile Rb+ ions can diffuse into perovskite film acting as a kind of passivation agent and suppressing ion migration. Herein, the synergy of mesoporous structure and RbF modification for SnO2 ETL was successfully achieved: not only the shortcoming of mesoporous structure in interfacial traps was overcome by RbF modification, but also the carrier collection efficiency was further improved. For the PSCs based on the m-SnO2/RbF ETL, a champion PCE of 22.72% along with a V OC of 1.139 V, J SC of 24.48 mA cm−2, and FF of 81.5% and a stability performance of maintaining 90% of the initial PCE after 300 h of MPP tracking were obtained. Considering no surface passivation of perovskite film was applied for those devices, those performances are outstanding indicating the great potential of the m-SnO2/RbF ETL for the application in high-efficiency and stable PSCs. Hence, utilizing the abovementioned synergy is an effective strategy for fabricating high-efficiency and stable PSCs. Since the fabrication process of the m-SnO2 ETL is a kind of low temperature process and RbF is very cheap, this strategy is also cost-effective and practical, making it of great value to promote the development of high-efficiency and stable PSCs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

We thank H.M. Li from Southwest Petroleum University for helping with the EQE measurements, and thank B. Cai from Sichuan Research Center of New Materials, China Academy of Engineering Physics for helping with the step profiler measurements.
